content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To      : File                                    Date  : Feb. 21, 1977


From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Ted Prim

Subject: Advice to Timothy Hayes.

After discussing his letter in the opinion request meeting
of February 14, I advised Mr. Hayes as follows: First, the
filer must disclose his pro rata share of the corporate in-
come without regard to whether the corporation is a DISC
or Subchapter S corporation. These are tax notions which
are irrelevant to the provisions of Section 82030.

Second, I advised him that clients must be disclosed only
where he knows or has reason to know of their identities.
Accordingly, when he operates through a broker and does
not know the names of the ultimate purchasers of his products,
he need not inquire as to their identities. However, if the
broker indicates to him that a person or company is his cus-
tomer, he would have to disclose that information.


TP:jo

TELEPHONE
707-442-1737

DAVID L. MOONIE & CO
CERTIFIED PUBLIC ACCOUNTANTS
350 E STREET
EUREKA, CALIFORNIA 95501

January 26, 1977

Mr. Ted Primm
Fair Political Practice Commission
1100 K. Street
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Mr. Primm,

     We are writing to request your advice concerning questions
that have arisen in connection with the filing of Form 721 - Statement
of Economic Interests.

     Our specific inquiry pertains to Form 721-C - Income of
Business Entities which do not Provide Legal or Brokerage Services,
as follows:

     1.  The instruction manual requires completion of the form
if a 10% or greater interest in a business entity is
owned.

     2.  The name of each person from whom the entity received
payments must also be disclosed if the pro rata share
of gross receipts was $10,000 or more.

     3.  The form requires the name of each individual or entity
from whom the business entity received payments if your
pro rata share of gross receipts from the individual or
entity was $10,000 or more.

     The circumstances involving our inquiry refer to an office
holder owning a 20% common stock interest in one closely-held corporation
and a 33 1/3% common stock interest in a second closely held corporation.
The office holder receives only salary and bonuses, the latter based
upon net income before taxes, and no dividends are paid by either
corporation.  Neither of these corporations are tax-option (Subchapter S)
type corporations where the net income passes through to the shareholders.
In this situation, where no portion of the gross receipts from any source
is paid or accrues the benefit of the shareholder, must a list be drawn-
up for each corporation that received in excess of $50,000 (for the 20%
corporate interest) and in excess of $30,000 (for the 33 1/2 % corporate
interest) from individuals and entities?

     The records of the corporations can probably produce the total
sales to specific parties in excess of the above limitations, although
it would be a time-consuming task, but it is our understanding that the
sales are owned by the corporation and not the shareholder, and the latter
has no claim on them.

Page 2
Mr. Ted Primm
January 26, 1977

     In reference to tax option corporations, which are not at
issue here, the question arises as to whether the federal tax code,
which passes net income through to shareholders, or the state codes,
which do not, are governing, in that for federal purposes, each
shareholder has the right to receive his pro-rata share of the net
profits derived from the operation of the corporation. To a certain
extent, a DISC passes through a portion of its net income for federal
purposes but the state of California does not recognize this form
of corporation, and the same situation evolves.

     We would very much appreciate your consideration of these
questions and advising this office as to the correct method of
completing this section of the form.

                                            Very truly yours,

                                            David L. Moonie & Co.

                                            By_________________________
                                               Timothy L. Hayes, CPA


cc: Mr. Christopher M. Ames




                                DAVID L.MOONIE & CO.
                                 CERTIFIED PUBLIC ACCOUNTANTS

sections.question:
s and advising this office as to the correct method of 
completing this section of the form. 
cc: 
Mr. Christopher M. Ames
sections.conclusion: First, the filer must disclose his pro rata share of the corporate income without regard to whether the corporation is a DISC or Subchapter S corporation. These are tax notions which are irrelevant to the provisions of Section 82030. Second, clients must be disclosed only where he knows or has reason to know of their identities. Accordingly, when he operates through a broker and does not know the names of the ultimate purchasers of his products, he need not inquire as to their identities. However, if the broker indicates to him that a person or company is his customer, he would have to disclose that information.
sections.question_synthetic: The filer seeks guidance on whether tax-based corporate structures (DISC and Subchapter S corporations) determine disclosure obligations under Section 82030, and whether client disclosure is required when the filer's identity of customers is unknown due to broker intermediation.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Tax classification of corporations is irrelevant to disclosure requirements under Section 82030; pro rata share must be disclosed regardless of corporate structure. Client disclosure is required only when the filer knows or has reason to know the client's identity; passive lack of knowledge through broker intermediation does not trigger disclosure obligations, but actual knowledge or broker indication does.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR document with clear Q/C structure embedded in memo format. Incoming request from David L. Moonie & Co. (dated Jan 26, 1977) followed by FPPC response memo (dated Feb 21, 1977) from Ted Prim. Formal sections follow typical advice letter format despite memo presentation.

cc: 
Mr. Christopher M. Ames

CONCLUSION: First, the filer must disclose his pro rata share of the corporate income without regard to whether the corporation is a DISC or Subchapter S corporation. These are tax notions which are irrelevant to the provisions of Section 82030. Second, clients must be disclosed only where he knows or has reason to know of their identities. Accordingly, when he operates through a broker and does not know the names of the ultimate purchasers of his products, he need not inquire as to their identities. However, if the broker indicates to him that a person or company is his customer, he would have to disclose that information.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises Timothy Hayes that corporate income disclosure under Section 82030 is independent of tax structures like DISC or Subchapter S corporations, and that client disclosure is required only when identities are known or reasonably discoverable, not through passive broker intermediation.
